schools.summary: Paul, beginning his self-defense, appeals emphatically 'I Paul myself' and entreats them 'by the meekness and gentleness of Christ' — the very graces of Christ he is accused of lacking, and which he wishes to imitate; citing the taunt of his opponents that 'in presence (face to face)' he is 'base (lowly, humble, unimpressive)' but 'being absent' (in his letters) is 'bold' — accused of cowardice in person and bravado at a distance (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).
Calvin: Paul takes up his opponents' sneer and turns it; his bodily lowliness is no weakness but the meekness of Christ, and his boldness is the authority God gave him for edification.

themes: I Paul myself beseech you by the meekness and gentleness of Christ
in presence am base among you, but being absent am bold toward you
Paul's appeal by Christ's meekness
answering the charge of inconsistency

schools.summary: Paul 'beseeches' them so to act that he 'may not be bold when present' — that he may not have to use, against them, the stern authority he is prepared to use 'against some'; these 'some' charge Paul with 'walking according to the flesh' — acting from worldly, carnal motives, by craft, ambition, or self-interest — and against such accusers Paul is ready to be bold (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Cyril and Calvin): Paul would rather come in gentleness, but against the obstinate slanderers who impugn his motives he will, if need be, exert his full apostolic authority.

themes: I may not be bold when I am present with that confidence... against some
which think of us as if we walked according to the flesh
Paul's wish not to need boldness
answering the charge of worldliness

schools.summary: Paul concedes 'we walk in the flesh' (live as men, in mortal bodies, subject to human weakness) but denies that 'we war after the flesh' — he does not wage his spiritual warfare by carnal, worldly methods; taking up the metaphor of warfare for the ministry, his conflict for the gospel is carried on not by fleshly weapons (craft, eloquence, force, worldly power) but by spiritual means (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the minister of Christ, though a frail man, must not fight Satan and error with carnal weapons but with the spiritual armour God supplies; the warfare answers to the weapons.

themes: though we walk in the flesh, we do not war after the flesh
Paul living in the flesh but not warring by carnal means
the spiritual warfare of the ministry
not fleshly weapons

schools.summary: Paul declares his 'weapons' (the means of his ministry) are 'not carnal' — not the eloquence, learning, wealth, force, or worldly power on which men rely — but spiritual: the truth of the gospel and the working of the Spirit; 'mighty (powerful) through God' (their efficacy not in themselves but in God, who accompanies them with the power of his Spirit); and mighty 'to the pulling down (demolishing) of strong holds' — the fortresses of error, sin, philosophy, idolatry, and pride entrenched against the gospel; the military figure used in the LXX for the capture of fortresses (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Calvin: whoever is unarmed with the influence of the Holy Spirit, however he boast the apostolic title, is no true minister; there is nothing in the world so strongly fortified — by cunning or by force — that God's spiritual armour cannot overthrow it, for Christ's kingdom is set up only by throwing down everything in the world that is exalted.

themes: the weapons of our warfare are not carnal, but mighty through God
to the pulling down of strong holds
spiritual weapons mighty through God
demolishing the fortresses of evil

schools.summary: The spiritual weapons cast down 'imaginations (reasonings, false philosophies, the devices of a wicked world)' and 'every high thing (every lofty pretension, proud opinion, rock-fortress of defence) that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God' — all that proudly resists the gospel — and complete the conquest by 'bringing into captivity (taking prisoner, as in war) every thought (every faculty of the mind, every reasoning) to the obedience of Christ'; the proud intellect is subdued and led captive to submit to him — Paul's conception of intellectual liberty, freedom in Christ (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): so long as we rest in our own judgment and are wise in our own estimation, we are far from Christ's doctrine; the liberty of the human mind must be restrained and bridled, the wise must become a fool (1 Corinthians 3:18), renouncing the wisdom of the flesh and, by the Spirit's guidance, allowing itself to be placed under control in a voluntary captivity of obedient faith.

themes: casting down imaginations, and every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God
bringing into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ
every proud thought brought captive to Christ
the surrender of the mind to obedient faith

schools.summary: Paul stands 'in a readiness to revenge (punish, avenge) all disobedience' — prepared, by his apostolic authority and discipline, to deal with the obstinate and rebellious who will not submit; but only 'when your obedience is fulfilled (completed)' — first the body of the church is to be brought to obedience, and then the few remaining rebels are to be dealt with, distinguishing the obedient majority from the disobedient (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul waits until the well-disposed have returned to obedience, that his severity may fall only on the obstinate, not on the whole church; discipline is exercised with discernment.

themes: having in a readiness to revenge all disobedience, when your obedience is fulfilled
Paul's readiness to punish disobedience
discipline reserved for the obstinate
the obedient distinguished from the rebellious

schools.summary: Paul challenges their judging 'after the outward appearance' — by surface, by the impressive bearing or claims of his rivals — rather than by reality (the clause read by some as a question, by some as a command, 'Look at what is before your eyes'); his point: if any (the rival teachers) confidently claims 'that he is Christ's' (a true minister, belonging to Christ), let him also reckon that 'as he is Christ's, even so are we Christ's' — Paul belongs to Christ as truly as any (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Henry, Calvin): Paul will not be reckoned inferior in belonging to Christ; whatever the rivals claim, he claims with at least equal right.

themes: Do ye look on things after the outward appearance
as he is Christ's, even so are we Christ's
Paul's claim to belong to Christ as truly as his rivals
not judging by appearance

schools.summary: Paul says that even 'if I should boast somewhat more of our authority' — claim still more of his apostolic power — he 'should not be ashamed,' for the claim is true and well-founded; the key qualification being that the authority was given by the Lord 'for edification (building up)' — to build the church — 'and not for destruction (pulling down)'; Paul's power, unlike a tyrant's, is for their good, not their harm (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the apostle's authority, however real and severe it may sometimes be, always aims at the church's edification; even his discipline is meant to save, not to ruin.

themes: our authority, which the Lord hath given us for edification, and not for your destruction
I should not be ashamed
authority given for edification, not destruction
power for the church's good

schools.summary: Paul adds this lest, in asserting his authority, he 'seem as if I would terrify (frighten) you by letters' — as though his stern letters were empty threats meant only to scare, with no real power behind them; taking up his opponents' taunt (stated in v.10) that his letters were weighty and frightening but his presence weak, he answers that his letters are no mere bluster, and he will be in deed what he is in word (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).

themes: That I may not seem as if I would terrify you by letters
Paul's care not to seem to frighten by letters
answering that his letters are no bluster
consistency of word and deed

schools.summary: Paul quotes his critics directly: 'his letters, say they, are weighty (impressive) and powerful (forceful)' — they admit the strength of his writing — 'but his bodily presence is weak, and his speech contemptible (of no account)'; the opponents disparaged Paul's personal appearance (perhaps unimpressive or infirm) and his manner of speaking (lacking the polished rhetoric prized by the Greeks), using this to undermine his authority — though the same authority that speaks in the letters belongs to the man (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).

themes: his letters, say they, are weighty and powerful; but his bodily presence is weak, and his speech contemptible
the opponents' taunt at Paul's presence and speech
despising the man while fearing the letters
the slander Paul answers

schools.summary: Paul's reply to the taunt: 'let such an one (the critic) think this' — let him be assured — that 'such as we are in word by letters when absent, such will we be also in deed when present'; there is no gap between his letters and his action. A quiet but firm warning: the boldness and authority of his letters are not empty; when he comes, he will act with the same firmness, and the critics should not presume on his supposed weakness in person (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: such as we are in word by letters when we are absent, such will we be also in deed when we are present
Paul's warning that he will be in deed what he is in word
no gap between letters and action
presume not on his supposed weakness

schools.summary: Paul says, with biting irony, that he 'dares not' rank or compare himself with those 'that commend themselves' — the rival teachers whose only credential is their own self-praise; their error being to take themselves (and each other) as the standard — 'measuring themselves by themselves, and comparing themselves among themselves' — instead of by God's measure, a closed circle of mutual flattery; and such self-comparison 'is not wise' (is foolish), producing only conceit and self-deception (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin: the true standard is not oneself or one's peers but God's calling and approval; those who measure themselves by themselves shut out the only measure that matters and deceive themselves.

themes: we dare not... compare ourselves with some that commend themselves
measuring themselves by themselves, and comparing themselves among themselves, are not wise
the folly of the self-commenders
self-comparison is not wisdom

schools.summary: Paul, unlike the self-commenders, 'will not boast of things without (beyond) our measure' — he will not claim what is not his — but only 'according to the measure of the rule (kanon, the assigned province, the limit) which God hath distributed (apportioned) to us'; the field God assigned him 'reaches even unto you' — Corinth fell within his God-given sphere, so he was their apostle by divine appointment, not an intruder (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: every minister should keep within the bounds of his calling, boasting only of what God has actually given him to do; Paul's boast is measured by God's apportionment.

themes: we will not boast of things without our measure, but according to the measure of the rule which God hath distributed to us
a measure to reach even unto you
boasting only within God's assigned measure
Corinth within Paul's God-given sphere

schools.summary: Paul 'stretches not himself beyond his measure (overextends not his claim) as though he reached not unto you' — he is not overreaching when he claims Corinth, for he genuinely 'came as far as to you also in preaching the gospel of Christ'; the decisive point being that Paul was the very one who first brought the gospel to Corinth (Acts 18), their founding apostle, so his authority over them is no presumption but his rightful sphere (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): it was by actually preaching Christ to them that Paul's claim was established — his apostleship among them is proved by their very existence as a church.

themes: we stretch not ourselves beyond our measure, as though we reached not unto you
we are come as far as to you also in preaching the gospel of Christ
Paul not overreaching, for he truly reached Corinth
their founding apostle

schools.summary: Paul, unlike his rivals, is 'not boasting of things without our measure, that is, of other men's labours' — he does not take credit for work done by others (as the intruders did, building on his foundation); his hope being that 'when your faith is increased' (when the Corinthians are confirmed and grown), he may be 'enlarged by you' — his sphere of labour extended through them, 'according to our rule (his assigned measure) abundantly' (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): Paul hopes that, with Corinth established, he may press the gospel into the regions beyond, using Corinth as a base — not poaching others' fields but breaking new ground.

themes: not boasting... of other men's labours
when your faith is increased, that we shall be enlarged by you according to our rule abundantly
not boasting in others' labours, but hoping for enlarged ministry
extending his own field

schools.summary: Paul's forward purpose: 'to preach the gospel in the regions beyond you' — to carry it into lands not yet evangelized, pressing into new territory; and 'not to boast in another man's line (sphere, kanon) of things made ready to our hand' — he will not glory in work already done by others, building on another's foundation; that was the intruders' way, not his (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with the grammatical-historical): Paul's holy ambition was to be a pioneer, breaking new ground for Christ where no one had labored (Romans 15:20) — not to reap where another had sown.

themes: to preach the gospel in the regions beyond you
not to boast in another man's line of things made ready to our hand
preaching in untouched regions, not another's field
the pioneer spirit

schools.summary: Paul quotes Jeremiah 9:24 (as in 1 Corinthians 1:31): 'he that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord' — all boasting is to be directed to the Lord, not to self or human attainment; this answers the self-commenders, whose glorying was in themselves, their own measure and labors, while the only legitimate glorying is in the Lord — in what God has done and given, ascribing all to him (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: all that the minister is and does is from God; therefore the only true boast is in the Lord, who alone gives the calling, the gifts, and the success.

themes: he that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord
the rule that all glorying must be in the Lord
boasting directed to God, not self
all the minister is comes from God

schools.summary: Paul's closing principle: 'not he that commendeth himself is approved' — self-praise proves nothing; the rival teachers' self-commendation does not make them approved ministers; the standard is 'whom the Lord commendeth' — God's commendation (shown by his calling, his gifts, and the fruit of his blessing on the ministry) is the only valid credential (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul's true commendation lay in the seal God set on his ministry (the Corinthians themselves, ch. 3), not in self-assertion; the test of a true minister is God's approval, not his own claims.

themes: not he that commendeth himself is approved, but whom the Lord commendeth
God's commendation, not self-commendation, approves
God's approval the only valid credential
the true test of the minister
